cards.sentence: it's very hard to cut through the noise these days. So you need humor is a great way to cut through it, but humor just for the sake of being funny is not that useful.

keyPhrase.meaning_ko: 정보가 넘쳐나는 환경에서 메시지를 돋보이게 해 주목을 끌어내다
keyPhrase.usageTip: Use this when describing the challenge of standing out in a competitive market or getting your pitch, product, or message noticed amid endless alternatives.

cards.context: Sam Lessin is discussing how founders can communicate effectively in an era of information overload. He argues that getting your message noticed requires more than just good content — you need deliberate strategies like humor, but only when it serves a real purpose.

cards.sentence: There's a rule, you always want to just double down in life. And so I'd say why etiquette? Look, there's a serious real narrative to why etiquette matters in 2025 for founders, almost 2026.

keyPhrase.meaning_ko: 의심을 받더라도 기존 방향에 더 강하게 베팅하다. '판돈을 두 배로 올리다'
keyPhrase.usageTip: Use this in business or strategy discussions when you or your team decide to invest more resources or commitment into a course of action rather than pulling back.

cards.context: Sam Lessin is explaining his personal philosophy of leaning harder into decisions rather than hedging. He uses this principle to justify his unconventional focus on etiquette as a serious topic for modern founders.

vocabulary.meaning: the story or reasoning used to explain and justify a position or strategy
vocabulary.note: In business, 'narrative' often refers to the compelling story a founder tells about why their company or idea matters.

cards.sentence: Understanding how to meet people, where they're at, build trust, mirror kind of expected behaviors. These are all tools.

keyPhrase.meaning_ko: 상대의 현재 상황·감정·관점에 맞춰 내 방식을 조율하다. 내 기준을 강요하지 않고 상대 눈높이로 내려가다
keyPhrase.usageTip: Use this when discussing stakeholder management, sales, or leadership — it signals empathy and adaptability, showing you tailor your communication style to your audience.

cards.context: Sam Lessin is making the case that social and interpersonal skills are practical tools for founders. He frames meeting people where they are — adapting to their perspective rather than imposing your own — as a core competency in building trust and influencing others.

vocabulary.meaning: to subtly reflect or match another person's communication style, tone, or behavior to build rapport
vocabulary.note: In negotiation and leadership contexts, mirroring is a deliberate technique to make others feel understood.

cards.sentence: there's a bunch of people around and the more you can be inclusive and low heart rate, it's not a scarcity mindset, it's an abundance mindset.

keyPhrase.meaning_ko: 기회와 성공이 한정돼 있다는 결핍 믿음. 나눠주면 내 몫이 줄어든다는 제로섬 심리
keyPhrase.usageTip: Use this in leadership or culture discussions to describe behavior driven by fear of losing out; pair it with 'abundance mindset' to frame the healthier alternative.

cards.context: Sam Lessin is contrasting two fundamental ways founders approach relationships and opportunities. He argues that staying calm and inclusive signals confidence, while anxious or exclusive behavior reflects a scarcity mindset — the belief that there is not enough to go around.

vocabulary.meaning: deliberately welcoming and making room for a wide range of people or perspectives
the belief that there is more than enough opportunity or success for everyone, enabling a generous and collaborative approach

cards.sentence: Yeah, I think you're creating an unnecessary uphill battery for yourself. I'd also say that, look, this goes back to technical differentiation where things are at.

keyPhrase.meaning_ko: 애초에 피할 수 있었던 장애물 때문에 훨씬 더 힘든 싸움을 하게 되다. 스스로 불리한 판을 만들다
keyPhrase.usageTip: Use this when explaining to a team or investor why a current approach is inefficient or unnecessarily difficult, and you are advocating for a smarter path.

cards.context: Sam Lessin is advising founders not to make their path to success harder than necessary. He warns that ignoring fundamentals like social skills or positioning creates avoidable obstacles — an uphill battle that drains energy better spent elsewhere.

vocabulary.meaning: the features or qualities that make a product, company, or approach meaningfully distinct from competitors

cards.sentence: I do think there's, again, leave them wanting more, I think is important. And that is partially about knowing when to excuse yourself gracefully as much as it is about when to enter.

keyPhrase.meaning_ko: 상대가 완전히 만족하기 전에 자리를 마무리해 다음 만남을 기대하게 만들다. '더 보고 싶다'는 여운을 남기다
keyPhrase.usageTip: Use this in sales, pitching, or networking contexts to describe the deliberate choice to end a meeting or presentation before exhausting your audience's interest.

cards.context: Sam Lessin is sharing etiquette advice for founders navigating social and professional events. He argues that knowing when to exit a conversation is just as strategically important as knowing how to enter one — ending on a high note makes people eager for the next interaction.

vocabulary.meaning: in a smooth, poised, and socially skillful manner, without causing awkwardness or offense

cards.sentence: there are lots of scenarios where I think an Irish goodbye is the best goodbye where you just kind of disappear.

keyPhrase.meaning_ko: 파티나 모임에서 인사 없이 조용히 사라지다. 작별 인사를 생략하고 자연스럽게 자리를 뜨다
keyPhrase.usageTip: Use this in casual conversation to describe a strategic or discreet exit from a social or professional situation; it lightens the mood while conveying deliberate social awareness.

cards.context: Sam Lessin is giving tactical advice on how to exit conversations and events gracefully. He makes the counterintuitive argument that sometimes the cleanest social move is a silent departure — the Irish goodbye — rather than a drawn-out farewell that creates awkwardness.

cards.sentence: the key is to give them plausible deniability to themselves and the community effectively that we've wound down this conversation. So you have to look for the signs.

keyPhrase.meaning_ko: 상대가 체면을 잃지 않고 물러설 수 있는 그럴듯한 명분을 만들어주다. '부인할 수 있는 여지'를 남겨 퇴로를 열어주다
keyPhrase.usageTip: Use this in negotiation, conflict resolution, or organizational contexts when describing how to give someone a face-saving way to change their position or exit a situation without losing credibility.

cards.context: Sam Lessin is teaching the social skill of gracefully ending conversations. He argues that simply walking away is not enough — a skilled communicator creates an off-ramp that lets the other person feel the conversation ended naturally, protecting their reputation and ego in the process.

vocabulary.meaning: seeming reasonable and believable, even if not fully verified
gradually brought to a close or conclusion in a calm, natural way

cards.sentence: It's like they're too capital. If they work, they're too capital intensive. Seed investing does not work in highly capital intensive businesses, so that's not going to happen.

keyPhrase.meaning_ko: 성과를 내기 전에 막대한 초기 자금이 필요한. 자본 소모가 큰
keyPhrase.usageTip: Use this in fundraising or strategy discussions to flag that a business model requires substantial upfront financial resources, which affects what kind of investors or funding structures are appropriate.

cards.context: Sam Lessin is explaining why certain technology categories are poorly suited for seed-stage investing. He argues that even if the business model works in theory, the sheer amount of capital required before a startup can prove itself makes it a bad match for early-stage funds.

vocabulary.meaning: providing early-stage funding to startups, typically before they have a fully proven product or revenue, in exchange for equity
vocabulary.note: Seed investors take on the highest risk and typically invest smaller amounts than later-stage venture funds.

cards.sentence: you're going to win because of something else, and AI is going to be a propellant to it.

keyPhrase.meaning_ko: 이미 갖고 있는 강점을 더 빠르고 강하게 밀어붙이는 추진제. 로켓 연료처럼 기존 우위를 폭발적으로 가속하는 요소
keyPhrase.usageTip: Use this as a vivid metaphor when describing how a technology, trend, or resource multiplies an existing strength rather than being the core strategy itself.

cards.context: Sam Lessin is sharing his view on AI's role in startup success. He argues that AI alone is not a sustainable competitive advantage — instead, founders win based on unique insights, relationships, or distribution, and AI simply accelerates whatever edge they already have.

vocabulary.meaning: something that drives rapid forward movement; here used metaphorically to mean a powerful accelerant for business growth
vocabulary.note: Literally, a propellant is the fuel in a rocket engine. The metaphor positions AI as rocket fuel for a startup's existing competitive advantage.

speaker.name: Sam Lessin
speaker.title: General Partner, Slow Ventures
speaker.titleHistory: VP of Product at Facebook · Two-time founder · General Partner, Slow Ventures
speaker.motif: a slowburning lantern and an open palm sigil
speaker.introLine: Every room you enter is a negotiation — the question is whether you know it.
speaker.sealLine: Go forth with a low heart rate, and the room will come to you.
